meta.title: Docker & Kubernetes
meta.description: Dockerfiles, container security, Kubernetes manifests, and deployment configs

items.text: Does the Dockerfile use multi-stage builds to minimize image size?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Multi-stage builds separate the build environment from the runtime image. Only copy the compiled output into the final stage.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Single-stage builds include compilers, dev dependencies, and source code — bloating images from 50MB to 1GB+ and increasing attack surface.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for multiple FROM statements. Verify the final stage uses a minimal base image and only copies built artifacts.
baseContent.exampleComment: This Dockerfile installs build tools in the final image. Use a multi-stage build with a builder stage and a slim runtime stage.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use multi-stage builds. Build in a full image, run in a minimal one.

items.text: Is the base image pinned to a specific version (not :latest)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Base images should use specific version tags (node:20.11-alpine) or SHA digests, not :latest or unpinned tags.
baseContent.whyItMatters: :latest changes without warning. A build that worked yesterday can break today because the base image updated.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check FROM statements for :latest or missing tags. Verify the tag is specific (includes minor/patch version).
baseContent.exampleComment: FROM node:latest is unpinned. Use FROM node:20.11-alpine for reproducible builds.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Pin base images to specific versions. Use alpine variants for smaller images.

items.text: Are Dockerfile layers ordered for optimal cache usage?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Put infrequently changing layers (OS packages, dependencies) before frequently changing ones (source code). Copy package files before source.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Docker rebuilds all layers after a changed layer. Copying source code before installing dependencies invalidates the dependency cache on every code change.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that COPY package.json + npm install comes before COPY . (source code). Verify system packages are installed early.
baseContent.exampleComment: COPY . . comes before npm install, invalidating the dependency cache on every code change. Copy package.json first, install, then copy source.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Order Dockerfile layers from least-changing to most-changing for optimal cache hits.

items.text: Does the container run as a non-root user?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Add a USER directive to run the process as a non-root user. Create a dedicated user with minimal permissions.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Running as root inside a container means a container escape gives root access to the host. Non-root limits blast radius.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for a USER directive in the Dockerfile. Verify the application doesn't require root (no binding to port 80, no writing to /etc).
baseContent.exampleComment: This Dockerfile runs as root by default. Add RUN adduser -D appuser && USER appuser before CMD.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always run containers as non-root. Create a dedicated application user.

items.text: Are secrets kept out of the image (no COPY .env, no ARG for secrets)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Never COPY secret files (.env, credentials) into the image. Don't pass secrets as build ARGs (visible in image history).
baseContent.whyItMatters: Secrets baked into images are visible to anyone who pulls the image. ARG values appear in docker history.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for COPY of .env files, credentials, or key files. Check for ARG used to pass secrets. Verify .dockerignore excludes secrets.
baseContent.exampleComment: This copies .env into the image. Remove it and pass secrets as runtime environment variables instead.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Never bake secrets into images. Use runtime environment variables or secret managers.

items.text: Is .dockerignore configured to exclude unnecessary files?

baseContent.whatItMeans: .dockerignore should exclude node_modules, .git, test files, docs, and other files not needed in the image.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without .dockerignore, COPY . . includes .git (huge), node_modules (should be rebuilt), and test files (unnecessary).
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for a .dockerignore file. Verify it excludes .git, node_modules, test/, docs/, and secret files.
baseContent.exampleComment: There's no .dockerignore. The build context includes .git (300MB) and node_modules. Add a .dockerignore file.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always have a .dockerignore. Exclude .git, node_modules, tests, and documentation.

items.text: Are CPU and memory resource requests and limits set?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Pods should define resources.requests (guaranteed) and resources.limits (maximum) for both CPU and memory.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without limits, a pod can consume all node resources, starving other pods. Without requests, the scheduler can't place pods correctly.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check container specs for resources.requests and resources.limits. Verify values are appropriate for the workload.
baseContent.exampleComment: This deployment has no resource limits. A memory leak will consume all node memory. Add requests and limits.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always set resource requests and limits. Monitor actual usage to tune them.

items.text: Are liveness, readiness, and startup probes configured?

baseContent.whatItMeans: livenessProbe restarts unhealthy pods. readinessProbe removes pods from service when not ready. startupProbe handles slow-starting apps.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without liveness probes, crashed processes stay running (zombie pods). Without readiness, traffic goes to unready pods.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for livenessProbe and readinessProbe in container specs. Verify probe endpoints, timing, and failure thresholds.
baseContent.exampleComment: This deployment has no readiness probe. During startup, traffic reaches the pod before it's ready, causing errors.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always configure readiness and liveness probes. Use startup probes for slow-starting applications.

items.text: Are replica counts and autoscaling appropriate?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Production deployments should have at least 2 replicas. Use HPA (Horizontal Pod Autoscaler) for variable load.
baseContent.whyItMatters: A single replica means downtime during deploys, node failures, or crashes. No autoscaling means over/under-provisioning.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check replica count (should be >= 2 for production). Verify HPA targets and min/max replicas if autoscaling is used.
baseContent.exampleComment: replicas: 1 for a production service. Set at least 2 and consider adding an HPA for traffic spikes.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Run at least 2 replicas in production. Use HPA for variable workloads.

items.text: Is the rolling update strategy configured correctly?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Rolling updates should set maxSurge and maxUnavailable. Use PodDisruptionBudget to ensure minimum availability during updates.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Wrong rolling update settings can cause downtime (all pods replaced at once) or very slow deployments.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check strategy type and maxSurge/maxUnavailable. Verify PodDisruptionBudget exists for critical services.
baseContent.exampleComment: maxUnavailable: 100% will replace all pods at once. Set maxUnavailable: 1 and maxSurge: 1 for zero-downtime deploys.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Configure rolling updates for zero downtime. Use PodDisruptionBudget for critical services.

items.text: Are labels and selectors consistent and meaningful?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use consistent labels (app, version, environment, team). Selectors must match pod labels exactly.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Mismatched selectors cause services to route to wrong pods or deployments to not manage their pods.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that Service selectors match Deployment pod template labels. Verify standard labels are applied consistently.
baseContent.exampleComment: The Service selector uses app: api but the Deployment labels use app: api-service. These must match.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Keep labels consistent. Verify selectors match pod template labels exactly.

items.text: Are security contexts configured (non-root, read-only, no privilege escalation)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Set securityContext with runAsNonRoot: true, readOnlyRootFilesystem: true, and allowPrivilegeEscalation: false.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without security contexts, containers run as root with writable filesystems, maximizing the impact of container escapes.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check pod and container securityContext. Verify runAsNonRoot, readOnlyRootFilesystem, and drop ALL capabilities.
baseContent.exampleComment: No securityContext defined. Add runAsNonRoot: true, readOnlyRootFilesystem: true, allowPrivilegeEscalation: false.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Set restrictive security contexts on all containers. Drop unnecessary Linux capabilities.

items.text: Are Kubernetes Secrets used correctly (not ConfigMaps for sensitive data)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Sensitive data must use Kubernetes Secrets, not ConfigMaps. Consider external secret managers (Vault, AWS Secrets Manager) for production.
baseContent.whyItMatters: ConfigMaps are not encrypted and are easier to accidentally expose. Secrets get basic base64 encoding and can be encrypted at rest.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for sensitive data in ConfigMaps. Verify Secrets are not committed to git. Check for external secret operator usage.
baseContent.exampleComment: The database password is in a ConfigMap. Move it to a Secret and consider using ExternalSecrets for production.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use Secrets for sensitive data. Use external secret managers for production workloads.

items.text: Are NetworkPolicies defined to restrict pod communication?

baseContent.whatItMeans: NetworkPolicies restrict which pods can communicate. By default, all pods can reach all other pods in the cluster.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without network policies, a compromised pod can access any service in the cluster, including databases and internal APIs.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for NetworkPolicy resources. Verify they restrict ingress and egress to only necessary communication.
baseContent.exampleComment: No NetworkPolicy exists. The API pod can directly access the database pod without going through the service. Add network policies.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Define NetworkPolicies for defense in depth. Restrict to only necessary communication paths.

items.text: Are container images scanned for vulnerabilities?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Container images should be scanned for known CVEs using tools like Trivy, Snyk, or registry-native scanning.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Base images contain OS packages with known vulnerabilities. Unpatched CVEs in production images are common attack vectors.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if the CI pipeline includes image scanning. Verify critical/high CVEs are addressed before deployment.
baseContent.exampleComment: This image hasn't been scanned. Add Trivy or Snyk to the CI pipeline to catch known vulnerabilities.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Scan images in CI. Block deployments with critical CVEs. Keep base images updated.

items.text: Is the correct Service type used (ClusterIP, NodePort, LoadBalancer)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: ClusterIP for internal services. LoadBalancer/Ingress for external access. NodePort rarely for development only.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Using LoadBalancer for internal services creates unnecessary cloud load balancers (cost). Using NodePort exposes ports on all nodes.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check Service types. Internal services should use ClusterIP. External services should use Ingress with ClusterIP backends.
baseContent.exampleComment: This internal database service uses type: LoadBalancer. Change to ClusterIP — it only needs to be reachable within the cluster.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use ClusterIP for internal, Ingress for external. Avoid LoadBalancer per-service — use an Ingress controller.

items.text: Is Ingress configured with TLS and appropriate annotations?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Ingress should terminate TLS, enforce HTTPS redirects, and have appropriate rate limiting and security headers.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Ingress without TLS exposes traffic in plaintext. Missing security headers enable clickjacking and other attacks.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for TLS configuration and cert-manager annotations. Verify HTTPS redirect is enabled.
baseContent.exampleComment: This Ingress has no TLS configuration. Add a tls section with a cert-manager ClusterIssuer for automatic certificate management.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always configure TLS on Ingress. Use cert-manager for automatic certificate management.

items.text: Are PersistentVolumeClaims sized and configured appropriately?

baseContent.whatItMeans: PVCs should have appropriate size, storage class, and access mode. Consider backup and resize strategies.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Undersized PVCs cause disk full errors. Wrong access mode prevents scheduling. No backup means data loss risk.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check PVC size against expected data growth. Verify storage class supports expansion if needed.
baseContent.exampleComment: This database PVC is 5Gi but the data grows 1Gi/month. Size it larger or ensure the StorageClass supports expansion.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Size PVCs for growth. Use expandable StorageClasses. Plan for backups.

items.text: Does the application log to stdout/stderr (not to files)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Containerized applications should log to stdout/stderr. Kubernetes captures these and routes them to the logging stack.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Logs written to files inside containers are lost when the container restarts. They also fill up the container filesystem.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check application logging configuration. Verify it writes to stdout/stderr, not to /var/log or other file paths.
baseContent.exampleComment: This app writes logs to /var/log/app.log. Configure it to log to stdout so Kubernetes can capture and forward them.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Log to stdout/stderr in containers. Let the platform handle log collection and routing.

items.text: Does the application handle SIGTERM for graceful shutdown?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Kubernetes sends SIGTERM before killing pods. Applications should handle it by finishing in-flight requests and cleaning up.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without graceful shutdown, in-flight requests are dropped, connections are broken, and data may be lost.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that the application handles SIGTERM. Verify terminationGracePeriodSeconds is long enough for cleanup.
baseContent.exampleComment: This Node.js app doesn't handle SIGTERM. Add a handler that stops accepting new requests and waits for in-flight ones.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Handle SIGTERM for graceful shutdown. Set terminationGracePeriodSeconds to match your shutdown time.
